ma_jayden.name: Ma Jayden
ma_jayden.summary:
A California competitor, Ma Jayden is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Ma's.

Ma competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
ma_jayden.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ma Jayden, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ma is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Ma has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ma saga began at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ma has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13, which means Ma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Ma entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Ma paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ma is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ma wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ma, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

maanas_mallela.name: Maanas Mallela
maanas_mallela.summary:
Representing Illinois, Maanas Mallela is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 3 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Maanas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Maanas has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Valarie Jiao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
maanas_mallela.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Maanas Mallela, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Maanas is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Maanas has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Maanas saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Maanas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Maanas has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Maanas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19, which means Maanas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Maanas keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Maanas has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Maanas, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Maanas. We will be here, taking notes.

madhav_karthikeyan.name: Madhav Karthikeyan
madhav_karthikeyan.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Madhav Karthikeyan has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Madhav's.

Notably, Madhav has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Madhav has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
madhav_karthikeyan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Madhav Karthikeyan, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Madhav has 2 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Madhav saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Madhav is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Madhav's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Madhav is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

At Junior Nationals, Madhav has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Madhav has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Madhav has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Madhav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Madhav entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Madhav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Madhav is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Madhav wants to be. madison_bach.name: Madison Bach
madison_bach.summary:
A California competitor, Madison Bach has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 29 entries in 10 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Madison is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Madison has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Emily Chan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (10 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jonathan Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Madison has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Madison has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Madison brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Emily Chan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Siddhant Pruthi has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Madison has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Madison is not just losing locally — Madison is losing NATIONALLY. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bach family travels cross-country so Madison can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Madison paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

We will be here, taking notes.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Madison entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Madison paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Madison keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Madison has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Madison, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Madison. We will be here, taking notes.

magadheesan_velu.name: Magadheesan Velu
magadheesan_velu.summary:
A Tennessee competitor, Magadheesan Velu is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in BD U11. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 7 career victories across 12 tournaments, Magadheesan has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Magadheesan has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Magadheesan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (BD U11) • 2 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Magadheesan boasts 48 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a career +560 point differential, a biggest win margin of 33 points.

Magadheesan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Riana Soprala (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Boqian Zheng (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

Magadheesan has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Magadheesan has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Magadheesan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Magadheesan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
magadheesan_velu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Magadheesan Velu, from Tennessee, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BD U11. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 34 career entries across 12 tournaments, 7 total wins, and 27 losses. A 21% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Magadheesan has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19), and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Magadheesan saga began at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Magadheesan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 7 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Magadheesan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Magadheesan is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Magadheesan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Magadheesan has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Magadheesan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, XD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Magadheesan does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Magadheesan has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 7 wins means Magadheesan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Magadheesan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Magadheesan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Magadheesan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Magadheesan's texts. The go-to partner is Riana Soprala (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Riana Soprala has emotionally recovered.

Of Magadheesan's 7 career titles, 4 came at local events versus 3 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Magadheesan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Then there is the seeding situation. Magadheesan has been seeded in 11 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Magadheesan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U13, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Magadheesan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Magadheesan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Magadheesan is not just losing locally — Magadheesan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 21% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Velu family travels cross-country so Magadheesan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Fortius South CRC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Magadheesan has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Magadheesan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U13, which means Magadheesan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Magadheesan entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Magadheesan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 34 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Magadheesan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Magadheesan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Magadheesan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Magadheesan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mahika_duggar.name: Mahika Duggar
mahika_duggar.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Mahika Duggar has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 17 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Mahika competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Mahika has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mahika brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mahika_duggar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mahika Duggar, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Mahika's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Mahika keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Mahika saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mahika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Mahika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mahika's texts. The go-to partner is Meghna Yeluguri (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15 — we hope Vihaan S Vinay has emotionally recovered.

Mahika has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mahika has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mahika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U15, which means Mahika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Mahika entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Mahika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Mahika keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Mahika has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Mahika, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Mahika. We will be here, taking notes.

mahith_jajula.name: Mahith Jajula
mahith_jajula.summary:
Out of Massachusetts, Mahith Jajula has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U11 level, capturing 2 titles across 9 tournaments and 21 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Mahith keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Mahith especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Mahith has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Summer Fei Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)
  • Harrell Thomas (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD)
  • Tejesh Talanki (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Mahith is still ascending.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mahith brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mahith_jajula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mahith Jajula, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 wins across 21 entries in 9 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Mahith shows up to a tournament, Mahith pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Mahith saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Wayside NE CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mahith's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mahith plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mahith can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Mahith has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Summer Fei Xu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U11 — we hope Arjun Kasu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mahith's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mahith has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mahith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Mahith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Mahith entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Mahith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 21 entries, and 19 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mahith looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mahith's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mahith has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mahith. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

manas_chintala.name: Manas Chintala
manas_chintala.summary:
Out of California, Manas Chintala stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

A true triple-threat, Manas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (11 entries) • 1 ORC title.

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Manas adapts to anyone:
  • Vignesh Jami — 6 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Prajwal Avadhani — a trusted BD partner (9 events, best: #2, spanning 5 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Manas has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Manas has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 12 seeded entries across the career, Manas consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Manas is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
manas_chintala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Manas Chintala, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 74 career entries in 30 tournaments, Manas has 3 titles — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 23 entries where Manas drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 74 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Manas saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Manas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Manas actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Manas is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Manas experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Manas has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Manas does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Manas has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 3 wins means Manas is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Manas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Manas is 0-for-26. Zero titles in 26 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Manas and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Manas needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Manas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Manas's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Prajwal Avadhani: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Prajwal Avadhani has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Aadya Pitani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Manas entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Manas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Manas and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Manas has shown up 11 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Manas is not just losing locally — Manas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chintala family travels cross-country so Manas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Manas has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Manas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Manas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Manas entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Manas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 74 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Manas looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Manas's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Manas has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Manas. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

manas_kathuria.name: Manas Kathuria

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Manas has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Manas brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
manas_kathuria.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Manas Kathuria, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Manas has 16 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Manas saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Manas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Manas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Manas's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Holly J. Fan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Manas has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Manas has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Manas has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Manas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Manas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Manas entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 97th. That is an average placement of 59th, which is a polite way of saying Manas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Manas keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Manas has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Manas, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Manas. We will be here, taking notes.

mandy_lu.name: Mandy Lu
mandy_lu.summary:
Representing New York, Mandy Lu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Mandy's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Mandy has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Mandy competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Mandy adapts to anyone:
  • Zechariah Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (13 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Aadya Pathak (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (7 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Bryce Xie (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Mandy has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Mandy has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Mandy is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
mandy_lu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mandy Lu, from New York, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mandy has 51 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mandy saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mandy's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 17 entries — a stretch where Mandy actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mandy is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Mandy would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 9 times Mandy has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 9 times Mandy has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Mandy's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mandy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Mandy is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Mandy and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Mandy needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Mandy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mandy's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Zechariah Wang: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Zechariah Wang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Zechariah Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mandy's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Mandy has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Then there is the seeding situation. Mandy has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Mandy takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in GS U11, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Mandy. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Mandy has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mandy is not just losing locally — Mandy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lu family travels cross-country so Mandy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mandy has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mandy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Mandy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Mandy entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Mandy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 51 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mandy looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mandy's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mandy has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mandy. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Afraz Muhammed Baig (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Callie Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Kameran Hossain (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U17 (3 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Nicholas Volfbeyn (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Manuel has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Manuel has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Manuel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
manuel_melwyn_alleluia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Manuel Melwyn Alleluia, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 73 career entries in 33 tournaments, Manuel has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 72 entries where Manuel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 73 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Manuel saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Manuel would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 8 times Manuel has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 8 times Manuel has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Manuel's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Manuel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 73 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 28 of them, to be precise. Manuel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Manuel's texts. The go-to partner is Afraz Muhammed Baig (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Ahwan Mishra has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Manuel entered 8 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Manuel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Manuel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Manuel has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Manuel has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Manuel is not just losing locally — Manuel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Alleluia family travels cross-country so Manuel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Manuel has 45 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Manuel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Manuel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Manuel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Manuel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 73 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Manuel. Manuel just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Manuel has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Manuel. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Manuel: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

manvi_jandhyala.name: Manvi Jandhyala
manvi_jandhyala.summary:
Representing Texas, Manvi Jandhyala has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Manvi's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Manvi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Manvi adapts to anyone:
  • Vedhanth Kumar Kanthala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Soudamini Reddy (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Manvi is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Manvi has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Manvi has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Manvi consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Manvi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
manvi_jandhyala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Manvi Jandhyala, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Manvi has 52 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Manvi saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Austin Leander South CRC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Manvi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Manvi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Manvi has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, where Manvi entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Manvi does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Manvi has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 2 wins means Manvi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Manvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Manvi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Manvi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Manvi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Xirui Wang: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Xirui Wang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Matthew Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Manvi's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Manvi has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Manvi a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Manvi has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in GS U13, where Manvi was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Manvi is not just losing locally — Manvi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jandhyala family travels cross-country so Manvi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Manvi has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Manvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Manvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Manvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Manvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 52 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Manvi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Manvi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Manvi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Manvi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

marco_castillo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Marco Castillo, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Marco is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South OLC, and so far, the only thing Marco has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Marco saga began at 2023 South OLC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Marco has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Marco has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Marco showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U19, which means Marco traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Marco entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Marco paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Marco is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Marco wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Marco, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Marcus has ZERO titles. Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Marcus plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 48 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Marcus has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Marcus's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aadya Pathak: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aadya Pathak has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Tamoghna Aniket Sivadanam has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Marcus has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Marcus a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Marcus has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in BS U11, where Marcus was seeded #2 and finished 17th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Marcus has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Marcus has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Marcus has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Marcus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Marcus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Marcus entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Marcus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Marcus keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Marcus has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Marcus, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Marcus. We will be here, taking notes.

marcus_ko.name: Marcus Ko
marcus_ko.summary:
A New York competitor, Marcus Ko knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 5 entries across 2 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Marcus competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Marcus has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Haoran Xia (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
marcus_ko.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Marcus Ko, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 5 entries in 2 tournaments. That is a 20% conversion rate, which means roughly 8 out of every 10 times Marcus shows up to a tournament, Marcus pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Marcus saga began at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC), where Marcus burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Marcus has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Marcus promised and what Marcus has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Marcus plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Marcus is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Marcus and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Marcus needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Marcus's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Marcus has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Marcus has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Marcus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19, which means Marcus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Marcus entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Marcus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Marcus is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Marcus wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Marcus, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.
