marissa_zhang.name: Marissa Zhang
marissa_zhang.summary:
Representing Washington, Marissa Zhang has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 24 tournaments and 62 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Marissa keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Marissa has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Marissa especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

With 22 different doubles partners over the years, Marissa adapts to anyone:
  • Calum Lim (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Sean Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Edwin Huang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Marissa is still ascending.

Marissa has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Marissa has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Marissa is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
marissa_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Marissa Zhang, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Marissa has 62 career entries across 24 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Marissa saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Marissa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Marissa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Marissa can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Marissa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Marissa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Steven Li: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Steven Li has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Sean Yang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Marissa's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Marissa has shown up 9 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Marissa has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Marissa is not just losing locally — Marissa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Marissa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Marissa has 46 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Marissa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Marissa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Marissa entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Marissa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 62 entries, and 60 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Marissa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Marissa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Marissa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Marissa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Mark has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mark has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mark showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Mark traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Mark entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Mark paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Mark keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Mark has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. We will be here, taking notes.

mark_w_wu.name: Mark w Wu
mark_w_wu.summary:
Out of Texas, Mark w Wu is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Mark is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Mark especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

Mark has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 23 different players across the career:
  • Diksha Chandrasekaran (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5)
  • Marzuq Mathani (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Renee Liu (current partner) — 5 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Advik Kevlani (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Mark has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mark has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 13 seeded entries across the career, Mark consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Mark has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
mark_w_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mark w Wu, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 81 career entries in 34 tournaments, Mark has 4 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Mark drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 81 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Mark saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mark's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Mark actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 18 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mark experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mark has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Mark does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Mark has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Mark is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mark plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mark can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Mark has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mark's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ariston Yu Feng Goh: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ariston Yu Feng Goh has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ksheera Karthik has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Mark entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Mark's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Mark and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Mark has shown up 5 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Mark of is playing it safe. Mark has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Mark has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Mark has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mark is not just losing locally — Mark is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Mark can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mark has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mark showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Mark traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Mark entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Mark paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 81 entries, and 77 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mark looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mark's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mark has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mark. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Marzuq is not just losing locally — Marzuq is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mathani family travels cross-country so Marzuq can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Marzuq has 41 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Marzuq showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Marzuq traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Marzuq entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Marzuq paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 82 entries, and 77 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Marzuq looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Marzuq's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Marzuq has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Marzuq. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mason_lee.name: Mason Lee
mason_lee.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Mason Lee has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 27 entries in 12 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Mason has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Mason especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Mason adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Mason has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mason_lee.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mason Lee, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mason has 27 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mason saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Mason has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mason's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Sriram Chandran has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Mason has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Mason has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Mason has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mason has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Mason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Mason entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Mason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Mason keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Mason has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Mason, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Mason. We will be here, taking notes.

matthew_alexander_lau.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Alexander Lau, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 wins across 18 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 17% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Matthew shows up to a tournament, Matthew pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Matthew saga began at 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Matthew's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Matthew can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Matthew has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Saanvi Jha (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Matthew's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Matthew is not just losing locally — Matthew is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lau family travels cross-country so Matthew can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U11, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 18 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Matthew looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Matthew's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Matthew has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Matthew. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A true triple-threat, Matthew competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior Championships competitor (2025) • U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Matthew adapts to anyone:

  • Venkata Shashank Kondaveeti (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Tahmid Hossain (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

A veteran of the junior circuit, Matthew has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Matthew has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Matthew consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 7 titles, Matthew has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
matthew_h_ponta.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew H Ponta, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. Hold on — Matthew has competed at the Pan American Junior Championships. That is an international tournament where you literally represent your country. Sounds amazing, right? Except Matthew finished with a best of top-17, which means Matthew flew to another country, wore the red-white-and-blue, stepped on court as a representative of the United States... and got bounced before the podium was even set up. Patriotism is beautiful. The results were not. Moving on to the other 63 career entries.

The Matthew saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Matthew's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 10 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Matthew experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Matthew has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Matthew does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Matthew has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 7 wins means Matthew is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Matthew can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Matthew has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Matthew's texts. The go-to partner is Aaron Fu (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Sophia Lin has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Matthew's 7 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Matthew's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Matthew and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Matthew has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Matthew of is playing it safe. Matthew has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Matthew has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Matthew has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Matthew is not just losing locally — Matthew is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ponta family travels cross-country so Matthew can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Matthew entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 57 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Matthew looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Matthew has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Neel Auro Chandra: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Neel Auro Chandra has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U19 — we hope Neel Auro Chandra has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Matthew has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Matthew has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U19, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Matthew keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Matthew has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Matthew, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Matthew. We will be here, taking notes.

matthew_lee.name: Matthew Lee
matthew_lee.summary:
Representing New York, Matthew Lee has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Matthew's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Matthew competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Matthew has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Matthew isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Matthew brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
matthew_lee.roast:

This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 17 career entries in 9 tournaments, Matthew has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Matthew drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 17 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Matthew saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Matthew has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Matthew's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Matthew and said 'nah.'

Matthew has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19), where Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Matthew keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Matthew has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Matthew, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Matthew. We will be here, taking notes.

matthew_lei.name: Matthew Lei
matthew_lei.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Matthew Lei is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U19) puts Matthew in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Matthew has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Matthew competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U19) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Matthew adapts to anyone:
  • Alan Premkumar (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Sydney Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Matthew has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Matthew has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Matthew has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
matthew_lei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Lei, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 50 career entries in 22 tournaments, Matthew has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 49 entries where Matthew drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 50 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Matthew saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Matthew's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 11 entries — a stretch where Matthew actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Matthew is 0-for-16. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Matthew is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Matthew and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Matthew needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Matthew has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Matthew's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tian Qi Alan Zhang: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tian Qi Alan Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Yuxuan Zeng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Matthew's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Matthew entered 8 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Matthew's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Matthew and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Matthew has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Matthew has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Matthew is not just losing locally — Matthew is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lei family travels cross-country so Matthew can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 50 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Matthew looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Matthew's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Matthew has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Matthew. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Matthew has 42 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Matthew saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Matthew has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Matthew's texts. The go-to partner is Jonathan Cai (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Meghna Ramakrishnan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Matthew has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Matthew has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

Matthew has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 42 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. matthew_paul_flowerhill.name: Matthew Paul Flowerhill
matthew_paul_flowerhill.summary: Out of California, Matthew Paul Flowerhill has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Matthew's.
matthew_paul_flowerhill.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Paul Flowerhill, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Matthew is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC), and so far, the only thing Matthew has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Matthew saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Matthew is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Matthew's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Matthew is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in BS U15, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Matthew is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Matthew wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Matthew, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

matthew_qiu.name: Matthew Qiu
matthew_qiu.summary:
Out of Texas, Matthew Qiu is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U13 level, with 5 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Matthew competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Matthew has formed effective partnerships:
  • Arthur Yu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
matthew_qiu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Qiu, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Matthew is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, and so far, the only thing Matthew has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Matthew saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC in BS U13, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Matthew keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Matthew has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Matthew, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Matthew. We will be here, taking notes.

matthew_wu.name: Matthew Wu
matthew_wu.summary:
Representing Texas, Matthew Wu keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U15 level, building a strong foundation across 17 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Notably, Matthew has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Matthew is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Matthew has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Derik S Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Matthew has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Matthew has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Matthew brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
matthew_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Wu, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 35 career entries in 17 tournaments, Matthew has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 34 entries where Matthew drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 35 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Matthew saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 35 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Matthew has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Matthew's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Zi Chen Shao has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from top-33 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Matthew's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Matthew and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Matthew has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Matthew has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Matthew is not just losing locally — Matthew is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Matthew can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Matthew entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 35 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Matthew. Matthew just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Matthew has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Matthew. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Matthew: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

matthew_zhang.name: Matthew Zhang
matthew_zhang.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Matthew Zhang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Matthew's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Matthew especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Matthew adapts to anyone:
  • Rheya S Kirikera (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Marcus K Chua (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events)
  • Theodore Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Matthew has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Matthew isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Matthew consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 4 titles, Matthew has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
matthew_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Matthew Zhang, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Matthew has 45 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Matthew saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Matthew's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Matthew actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 10 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Matthew plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Matthew can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Matthew has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Matthew's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Felix Liu: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Felix Liu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Felix Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Matthew's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Matthew has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Then there is the seeding situation. Matthew has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Matthew takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in BS U13, and finishing 3rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Matthew. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Matthew has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Matthew has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Matthew showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11, which means Matthew traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Matthew entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Matthew paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 45 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Matthew looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Matthew's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Matthew has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Matthew. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Either nobody wants to be Maurvi's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Maurvi is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Maurvi has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Maurvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in GS U13, which means Maurvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Maurvi is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Maurvi wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Maurvi, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Out of Texas, Max Chiu keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U11 level, building a strong foundation across 7 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Max is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Max adapts to anyone:
  • Jeffrey Yan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Max brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
max_chiu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Max Chiu, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Max has 16 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Max saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Max plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Max has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Jeffrey Yan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Chris Lin has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Max has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Max has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Max showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Max traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Max entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Max paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Max keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Max has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Max, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Max. We will be here, taking notes.
