max_zhang.name: Max Zhang
max_zhang.summary:
Hailing from California, Max Zhang has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 BD, U15 BS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 6 career titles across 21 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Max has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Max is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 BD, U15 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U13, BS U13, BD U15, BD U13) • 4 ORC titles.

Max has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 18 different players across the career:
  • Layton Wen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Justin Liaw — 3 events in BD/U17, best: #2

Having moved up to U19, Max is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Max brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Max has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Max has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 14 seeded entries across the career, Max consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 6 titles, Max has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
max_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Max Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Max is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Max has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Max would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Max, we kept reading — all 61 entries across 21 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 10% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Max held up a trophy, there were roughly 9 other times Max walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Max saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Max's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 4 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Max actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Max is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Max experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Max has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 SoCal ORC, where Max entered 5 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Max does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Max has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 6 wins means Max is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Max plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Max can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Max has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Max's texts. The go-to partner is Vivek Rai (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Brianna Lee has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Max has competed 11 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Max a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Max has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, where Max was seeded #1 and finished 13th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Max has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Max is not just losing locally — Max is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Max can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Max has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Max showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Max traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Max entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Max paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Max looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Max's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Max has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Max. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

maximus_nguyen.name: Maximus Nguyen
maximus_nguyen.summary:
A California competitor, Maximus Nguyen has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 23 entries across 10 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Maximus is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries).

Maximus has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Felicia Ho (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #3)
  • Ethan Chan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U17 (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Maximus is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Maximus is still ascending.

Maximus has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Maximus has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Maximus's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
maximus_nguyen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Maximus Nguyen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 23 career entries in 10 tournaments, Maximus has 1 title — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 22 entries where Maximus drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 23 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Maximus saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Maximus's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Maximus plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Maximus is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Maximus and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Maximus needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Maximus has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Maximus's texts. The go-to partner is Felicia Ho (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope William Runyan Ding has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Maximus's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Maximus entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Maximus's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Maximus and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Maximus is not just losing locally — Maximus is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Nguyen family travels cross-country so Maximus can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Maximus has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Maximus showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Maximus traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Maximus entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Maximus paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 23 entries, and 22 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Maximus looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Maximus's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Maximus has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Maximus. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

maya_lekkala.name: Maya Lekkala
maya_lekkala.summary:
Representing Texas, Maya Lekkala is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 14 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Maya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Maya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Ian Darmawan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Maya has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Maya's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
maya_lekkala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Maya Lekkala, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Maya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Maya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Maya saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Maya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Maya has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Ian Darmawan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U13 — we hope Shrivaishnavi Jami has emotionally recovered.

Maya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Maya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Maya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Maya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Maya entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Maya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Maya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Maya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Maya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Maya. We will be here, taking notes.

The Md. saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Medha saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

medha_pediredla.name: Medha Pediredla
medha_pediredla.summary:
Out of Texas, Medha Pediredla embodies the relentless spirit of junior badminton, currently competing at the U15 level, with 39 career entries across 14 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Medha's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

Medha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Medha has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Medha has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Medha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
medha_pediredla.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Medha Pediredla, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 39 career entries in 14 tournaments, Medha has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 38 entries where Medha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 39 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Medha saga began at 2022 South Frisco U11 OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Medha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Medha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Medha's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Harshith Yoganand has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Medha entered 4 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Medha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Medha and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Medha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Medha is not just losing locally — Medha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pediredla family travels cross-country so Medha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Medha has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Medha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Medha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Medha entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Medha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Medha. Medha just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Medha has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Medha. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Medha: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

medha_sivani_gudavalli.name: Medha Sivani Gudavalli
medha_sivani_gudavalli.summary:
Hailing from Virginia, Medha Sivani Gudavalli has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Medha has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Medha competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 6 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Diving into the match data reveals: Medha boasts a biggest win margin of 30 points, a solid 63% win rate over 81 matches, a positive +223 point differential across the career.

Medha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Liam Xiao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (9 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Catherine Du (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #3)
  • Lukas Gabriel Bayani (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Zmy Gao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Medha is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Medha has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Medha has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Medha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
medha_sivani_gudavalli.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Medha Sivani Gudavalli, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 70 career entries in 24 tournaments, Medha has 7 titles — a 10% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 9 entries where Medha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 70 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Medha saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U11), where Medha burst onto the scene with a title in GD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Medha has delivered on some of those promises: 7 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Medha promised and what Medha has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 27 entries — a stretch where Medha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 18 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Medha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Medha has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Medha does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Medha has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 7 wins means Medha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Medha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Medha is 0-for-23. Zero titles in 23 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Medha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 7 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Medha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Medha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Medha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Liam Xiao: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Liam Xiao has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Liam Xiao has emotionally recovered.

Of Medha's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Medha feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Medha entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Medha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Medha and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Medha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Medha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Medha is not just losing locally — Medha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gudavalli family travels cross-country so Medha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Medha has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Medha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15, which means Medha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Medha entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Medha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 70 entries, and 63 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Medha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Medha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Medha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Medha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

meera_vinodh.name: Meera Vinodh
meera_vinodh.summary:
Meera Vinodh is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U11 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Meera competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
meera_vinodh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Meera Vinodh, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Meera is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Meera has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Meera saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Meera plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Meera has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Meera showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Meera traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Meera entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Meera paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Meera is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Meera wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Meera, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

megan_tai.name: Megan Tai
megan_tai.summary:
Representing Illinois, Megan Tai has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 8 entries across 4 tournaments, Megan is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Megan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Megan has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Victoria Vy Dinh (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
megan_tai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Megan Tai, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Megan has 8 career entries across 4 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Megan saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Megan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Megan has reached the finals 2 times — at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC, 2023 Midwest OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Megan has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Megan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Megan has shown up 2 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Megan has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Megan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19, which means Megan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Megan entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 25th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Megan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Megan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Megan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Megan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

meggie_chang.name: Meggie Chang
meggie_chang.summary:
A Maryland competitor, Meggie Chang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Meggie's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Meggie has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Meggie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Meggie adapts to anyone:
  • Geoffray Lee (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Meggie has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Meggie has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Meggie consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Meggie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
meggie_chang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Meggie Chang, from Maryland, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 13 tournaments, Meggie has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Meggie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Meggie saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Meggie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 38 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Meggie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Meggie's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Carissa M Chen: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Carissa M Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U15 — we hope Zeyue Du has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Meggie entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Meggie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Meggie and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Meggie has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Meggie has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Meggie is not just losing locally — Meggie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chang family travels cross-country so Meggie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Meggie has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Meggie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Meggie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Meggie entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Meggie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 38 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Meggie. Meggie just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Meggie has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Meggie. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Meggie: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

megha_challa.name: Megha Challa
megha_challa.summary:
Hailing from New Jersey, Megha Challa has established a commanding presence at the local tournament level, currently competing at the U17 level, racking up 5 titles across 12 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Megha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Megha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Aashi Gupta (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (6 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Alex Wen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Alexander Gu (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jessica Zhang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

Megha has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Megha isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Megha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
megha_challa.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Megha Challa, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 33 career entries in 12 tournaments, Megha has 5 titles — a 15% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 5 entries where Megha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 33 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Megha saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Megha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Megha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Megha is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Megha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Megha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Megha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Megha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aashi Gupta: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aashi Gupta has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Aashi Gupta has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Megha's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Megha entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Megha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Megha and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Megha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Megha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Megha has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Megha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U15, which means Megha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Megha entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Megha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 33 entries, and 28 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Megha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Megha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Megha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Megha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Out of Illinois, Meghana Dixit has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 7 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Meghana competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Notably, Meghana has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Meghana is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Meghana adapts to anyone:
  • Mihir Thaha (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (22 events, best: #3, spanning 6 seasons)
  • Daby Huang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Radhika Birur (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Savannah Kam — a trusted GD/U17 partner (4 events, best: #5)

Meghana has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Meghana has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 6 seasons and 2 titles, Meghana has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
meghana_nishant.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Meghana Nishant, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 76 career entries in 27 tournaments, Meghana has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Meghana drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 76 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Meghana saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Meghana's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Meghana actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Meghana is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Meghana would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 11 times Meghana has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 11 times Meghana has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Meghana's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Meghana plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Meghana is 0-for-26. Zero titles in 26 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Meghana and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Meghana needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Meghana has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Meghana's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Mihir Thaha: 22 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 22 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Mihir Thaha has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Mihir Thaha has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Meghana entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Meghana's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Meghana and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Meghana 12 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Meghana has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Meghana is not just losing locally — Meghana is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Nishant family travels cross-country so Meghana can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Meghana has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Meghana showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Meghana traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Meghana entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Meghana paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 76 entries, and 74 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Meghana looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Meghana's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Meghana has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Meghana. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Meghna saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

meghna_ramakrishnan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Meghna Ramakrishnan, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Meghna's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Meghna keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Meghna saga began at 2025 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Meghna has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Matthew Luo has emotionally recovered.

Meghna has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Meghna has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Meghna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Meghna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Meghna entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Meghna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Meghna, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Meghna. We will be here, taking notes.

meghna_yeluguri.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Meghna Yeluguri, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. The Meghna saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Meghna experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Meghna has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Fortius South CRC, 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Meghna has it in spades.

And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Meghna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Meghna's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sankeerthi Marasani: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sankeerthi Marasani has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Kyler Trice has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Meghna has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Meghna has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Meghna has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Meghna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Meghna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Meghna entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Meghna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 50 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Meghna. Meghna just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Meghna has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Meghna. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Meghna: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Mehan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.
