melvin_cui.name: Melvin Cui
melvin_cui.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Melvin Cui is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (BS U13, BD U13) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 14 career titles across 34 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Melvin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Melvin competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 9 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BS U13, BD U13, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U13 BD, U13 BS) • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U13, BD U11, BD U13) • 7 ORC titles.

With 27 different doubles partners over the years, Melvin adapts to anyone:
  • Aditya Namboodiripad (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (11 total events in BD/U13, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Melvin is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Melvin has competed across U11, U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Melvin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Melvin has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 14 titles, Melvin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
melvin_cui.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Melvin Cui, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Melvin is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BS U13, BD U13), which means Melvin has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Melvin's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 91 career entries to discuss and a 15% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Melvin saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco U11 OLC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Melvin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 8 titles in 29 entries — a stretch where Melvin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Melvin is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Melvin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Melvin has reached 11 championship matches across this career. That is 11 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 11 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), where Melvin entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (6 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Melvin does own 14 actual titles, so it is not like Melvin has never won anything. But 11 finals losses versus 14 wins means Melvin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Melvin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 9 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Melvin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 27 of them, to be precise. Melvin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Melvin's texts. The most successful partnership is with Aditya Namboodiripad — 11 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Aditya Namboodiripad deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Aditya Namboodiripad a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U13 — we hope Bella Liu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Melvin entered 3 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Melvin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Melvin and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Melvin 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Melvin has been seeded in 23 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Melvin takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U13, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Melvin. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Melvin of is playing it safe. Melvin has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Melvin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 9 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Melvin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Melvin is not just losing locally — Melvin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cui family travels cross-country so Melvin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Melvin has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Melvin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15, which means Melvin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Melvin entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Melvin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 14 titles to the name, 91 entries, and 77 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Melvin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Melvin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Melvin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Melvin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

melvin_o_thu.name: Melvin O Thu
melvin_o_thu.summary:
Representing New York, Melvin O Thu is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 11 tournaments and 17 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Melvin keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Melvin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (1 entry).

Melvin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Haoyang Zheng (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Melvin is peaking at the right time in 2023-2024, with 2 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Melvin is still ascending.

Melvin has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, South, Melvin isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Melvin is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
melvin_o_thu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Melvin O Thu, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 17 career entries in 11 tournaments, Melvin has 2 titles — a 12% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Melvin drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 17 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Melvin saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best finish of 49th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Melvin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2023-2024 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 4 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Melvin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Melvin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Melvin has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U17 — we hope Benjamin Dictado has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Melvin's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Melvin entered 4 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Melvin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Melvin and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Melvin has shown up 1 time with a best of top-49. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Melvin has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Melvin has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Melvin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Melvin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Melvin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Melvin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 17 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Melvin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Melvin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Melvin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Melvin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

merrisa_wang.name: Merrisa Wang
merrisa_wang.summary:
Hailing from Pennsylvania, Merrisa Wang has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 18 entries in 7 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Merrisa is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Merrisa competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Merrisa has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Michael Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Melanie Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Merrisa has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Merrisa brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
merrisa_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Merrisa Wang, from Pennsylvania, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 18 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 18-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Merrisa's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Merrisa keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Merrisa saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Merrisa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 18 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Merrisa has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Michael Li: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Michael Li has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15 — we hope Michael Li has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Merrisa of is playing it safe. Merrisa has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Merrisa has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Merrisa has built a permanent residence on that line. Merrisa has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Merrisa has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Merrisa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13, which means Merrisa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), where Merrisa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Merrisa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Merrisa keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Merrisa has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Merrisa, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Merrisa. We will be here, taking notes.

The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mia_k_hundley.name: Mia K Hundley
mia_k_hundley.summary:
Out of California, Mia K Hundley wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in GD U15, XD U15. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 3 career victories across 7 tournaments, Mia has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

What makes Mia especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (GD U15, XD U15) • 1 ORC title.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Mia adapts to anyone:
  • Emma Wei (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Michael Jiang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Jack Yin (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2021-2022 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Mia is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Mia has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Mia has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Mia consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Mia's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
mia_k_hundley.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mia K Hundley, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U15, XD U15. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 19 career entries across 7 tournaments, 3 total wins, and 16 losses. A 16% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Mia has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Mia saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Mia burst onto the scene with a title in XD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Mia has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Mia promised and what Mia has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2020-2021 season — 2 titles in 3 entries — a stretch where Mia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 16 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mia experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mia has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Mia entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Mia does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Mia has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 3 wins means Mia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Mia is 0-for-5. Zero titles in 5 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Mia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Mia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Mia has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Emma Wei (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Michael Jiang has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mia is not just losing locally — Mia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 16% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hundley family travels cross-country so Mia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mia has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Mia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Mia entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Mia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 19 entries, and 16 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mia looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mia's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mia has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mia. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Mia especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Mia has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Alice Y Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17/U19 (13 events, best: #4, spanning 4 seasons)

Mia has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Mia has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Mia brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mia_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mia Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 29 career entries in 14 tournaments, Mia has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 28 entries where Mia drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 29 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Mia saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Alice Y Wang: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alice Y Wang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Daniel Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Mia entered 6 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Mia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Mia and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Mia 7 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mia is not just losing locally — Mia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Mia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-25 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mia has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Mia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Mia entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Mia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Mia. Mia just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Mia has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Mia. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Mia: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

micah_cruz.name: Micah Cruz
micah_cruz.summary:
Hailing from New York, Micah Cruz has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 GS, U19 XD, U19 GD, U19 GS, U15 XD, U15 GD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 19 career titles across 15 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Micah is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Micah competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 8 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GS, U19 XD, U19 GD, U19 GS, U15 XD, U15 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U13, GS U13, GS U17, GD U15) • 14 ORC titles.

Micah has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Sydney Li (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD/U19
  • Rylan Tan (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 4 events in U15/U19/XD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Mikayla Cruz — 3 events, 1 title together in GD
  • Shriyans Bhagavatula — 6 events, 1 title together in U15/XD, across 2 seasons

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 2 tournaments. Micah is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Micah has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, SoCal, South, Micah isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Micah has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 19 titles, Micah is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
micah_cruz.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Micah Cruz, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Micah is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Micah has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Micah would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Micah, we kept reading — all 42 entries across 15 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 45% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Micah held up a trophy, there were roughly 1 other times Micah walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Micah saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Micah burst onto the scene with a title in GS U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Micah has delivered on some of those promises: 19 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Micah promised and what Micah has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 8 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Micah actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 5 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Micah experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Micah has reached 10 championship matches across this career. That is 10 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 10 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Micah entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (U15 GS, U15 XD, U15 GD). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Micah does own 19 actual titles, so it is not like Micah has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 19 wins means Micah is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Micah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 8 singles, 4 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Micah can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Micah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Micah's texts. The go-to partner is Brianna Lee (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire.

Then there is the seeding situation. Micah has been seeded in 13 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Micah takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in GS U15, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Micah. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Micah of is playing it safe. Micah has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Micah has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 16 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Micah has built a permanent residence on that line. Micah has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 19 titles to the name, 42 entries, and 23 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Micah looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Micah's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Micah has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Micah. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Micah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 75 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Micah has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Micah's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Raymond Haiwen Song: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Raymond Haiwen Song has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Luna Han has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Micah 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Micah of is playing it safe. Micah has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Micah has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Micah has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Micah is not just losing locally — Micah is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wei family travels cross-country so Micah can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Micah has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Micah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Micah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Micah entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Micah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 75 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Micah. Micah just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Micah has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Micah. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Micah: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

michael_chen.name: Michael Chen
michael_chen.summary:
Representing California, Michael Chen is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Michael is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Michael has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Aiden Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #3)

Michael has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Michael has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Michael brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
michael_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michael Chen, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Michael has 31 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Michael saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Michael would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Michael has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Michael has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Michael's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michael plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 31 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Michael has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Michael's texts. The go-to partner is Aiden Wu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Ryan Li has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Michael has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Michael is not just losing locally — Michael is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Michael can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michael has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Michael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Michael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Michael keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Michael has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Michael, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Michael. We will be here, taking notes.

michael_jiang.name: Michael Jiang
michael_jiang.summary:
A California competitor, Michael Jiang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Michael's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Michael competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Michael has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Jaden Jin — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Michael has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Michael has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Michael consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Michael is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
michael_jiang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michael Jiang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 16 tournaments, Michael has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Michael drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Michael saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Michael's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Michael actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Michael is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Michael experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Michael has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal Arena CRC, where Michael entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17, BD U17). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Michael does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Michael has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 1 wins means Michael is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michael plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Michael is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Michael and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Michael needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Michael has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Michael's texts. The go-to partner is Jaden Jin (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Kody Seow has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Michael's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Michael's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Michael and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Michael has shown up 5 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Michael is not just losing locally — Michael is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jiang family travels cross-country so Michael can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michael has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Michael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Michael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 38 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Michael looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Michael's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Michael has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Michael. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Michael has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Michael, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Michael has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Michael has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Michael is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
michael_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michael Liu, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Michael has 42 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Michael saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 MassBad NE OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Michael's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Michael actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Michael is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Michael would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Michael has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Michael has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Michael's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michael plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Michael is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Michael and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Michael needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Michael has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Michael's texts. The go-to partner is Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan (10 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Michael's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Michael 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Michael has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Michael is not just losing locally — Michael is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Michael can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michael has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Michael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Michael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 42 entries, and 40 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Michael looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Michael's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Michael has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Michael. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Michael especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Alicia Chang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Michael has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, South, Michael isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Michael brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Michael. Michael just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Michael has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what?
