At Junior Nationals, Michael has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Michael has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michael has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Michael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 ABC NorCal OLC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Michael entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Michael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Michael keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Michael has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Michael, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Michael. We will be here, taking notes.

michael_yufu_sun.name: Michael Yufu Sun
michael_yufu_sun.summary:
A California competitor, Michael Yufu Sun is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Michael in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Michael is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Michael especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U13 XD, U13 BS) • Junior Nationals top-4 (BS U13) • 8 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Michael boasts 54 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a biggest win margin of 34 points, an extraordinary 82% match win rate across 78 matches.

Michael has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Vignan Chandrasekaran (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Grace Cheng (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD)
  • Sophia Liu — a trusted U13/XD partner (4 events, best: #2)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 6 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Michael is still ascending.

Michael has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Michael has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Michael consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Michael's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
michael_yufu_sun.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michael Yufu Sun, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 33 career entries in 11 tournaments, Michael has 8 titles — a 24% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 3 entries where Michael drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 33 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Michael saga began at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Michael burst onto the scene with a title in BD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Michael has delivered on some of those promises: 8 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Michael promised and what Michael has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Michael experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Michael has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Michael entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Michael does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Michael has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 8 wins means Michael is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michael plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Michael can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Michael has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The most successful partnership is with Vignan Chandrasekaran — 6 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Vignan Chandrasekaran deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Vignan Chandrasekaran a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Michael's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Michael and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Michael 3 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Michael of is playing it safe. Michael has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Michael has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Michael has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Michael is not just losing locally — Michael is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 24% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sun family travels cross-country so Michael can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michael has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15, which means Michael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Michael entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 65th. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Michael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 33 entries, and 25 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Michael looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Michael's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Michael has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Michael. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Representing California, Michelle Yourong Zhang has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 GD, U15 GS, U15 XD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 6 career titles across 25 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Michelle is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Michelle especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Michelle has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Vivian Feng (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (8 total events in GD/U15, across 2 seasons)
  • Kevin Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3)

Having moved up to U19, Michelle is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Michelle brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Michelle has competed across U11, U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Michelle has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Michelle is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
michelle_yourong_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michelle Yourong Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Michelle is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Michelle has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Michelle would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Michelle, we kept reading — all 70 entries across 25 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 9% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Michelle held up a trophy, there were roughly 10 other times Michelle walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Michelle saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal ORC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Michelle's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 4 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Michelle actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Michelle is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Michelle experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Michelle has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Michelle entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Michelle does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Michelle has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 6 wins means Michelle is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michelle plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Michelle is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Michelle and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Michelle needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Michelle has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Michelle's texts. The go-to partner is Selena Wei (9 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Tsun Him Lam has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Michelle 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Michelle has been seeded in 7 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Michelle takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U15, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Michelle. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Michelle of is playing it safe. Michelle has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Michelle has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 6 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Michelle has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Michelle is not just losing locally — Michelle is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Michelle can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Michelle has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Michelle showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13, which means Michelle traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Michelle entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Michelle paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 70 entries, and 64 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Michelle looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Michelle's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Michelle has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Michelle. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

michelle_zhang.name: Michelle Zhang
michelle_zhang.summary:
Out of Illinois, Michelle Zhang is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U15, XD U15, GS U15) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 2 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Notably, Michelle has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Michelle competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U15, XD U15, GS U15, 2025).

Michelle has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Vivian Feng (current partner) — 2 events in GD, producing 2 titles together
  • Terence Cai (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)

Michelle is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 2 titles across 1 tournaments — a clear sign that Michelle is still ascending.

michelle_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Michelle Zhang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Michelle is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U15, XD U15, GS U15), which means Michelle has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Michelle's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 7 career entries to discuss and a 29% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Michelle saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Pan Am Junior in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Michelle's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 5 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Michelle experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Michelle has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Pan Am Junior, where Michelle entered 5 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U15, XD U15, XD U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Michelle does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Michelle has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Michelle is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Michelle plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Michelle is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Michelle and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Michelle needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Michelle has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 7 entries, and 5 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Michelle looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Michelle's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Michelle has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Michelle. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Mihika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 78 losses. Mihir takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U19, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Mihir. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Mihir has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mihir has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mihir showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U19, which means Mihir traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 14 entries, and 12 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mihir looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mihir's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mihir has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mihir. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mihir_thaha.name: Mihir Thaha
mihir_thaha.summary:
Representing California, Mihir Thaha is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (XD U17, BD U17) puts Mihir in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Mihir has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Mihir competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 4 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U17, BD U17) • 1 ORC title.

Mihir has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Pradyun Gowda (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (28 total events in BD/U15/U17, spanning 6 seasons)
  • Meghana Nishant (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (22 events, best: #3, spanning 6 seasons)

Mihir has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mihir has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Mihir has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 7 titles, Mihir has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
mihir_thaha.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mihir Thaha, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 82 career entries in 30 tournaments, Mihir has 7 titles — a 9% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 10 entries where Mihir drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 82 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Mihir saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mihir's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 25 entries — a stretch where Mihir actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mihir is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mihir experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mihir has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Mihir does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Mihir has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 7 wins means Mihir is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mihir plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 3 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mihir can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. The most successful partnership is with Pradyun Gowda — 28 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Pradyun Gowda deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Pradyun Gowda a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Meghana Nishant has emotionally recovered.

Of Mihir's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Mihir feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Mihir entered 7 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Mihir's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Mihir and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Mihir has competed 12 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Mihir a #1 or #2 seed 5 times, and Mihir has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in BS U15, where Mihir was seeded #2 and finished 9th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Mihir has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mihir is not just losing locally — Mihir is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Thaha family travels cross-country so Mihir can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mihir has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mihir showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Mihir traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Mihir entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Mihir paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 82 entries, and 75 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mihir looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mihir's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mihir has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mihir. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mikael_chang.name: Mikael Chang
mikael_chang.summary:
Representing California, Mikael Chang is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (XD U15). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Mikael in rarified international company.

Mikael is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (XD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U15 XD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (BS U13, BD U15, XD U15, BD U13) • 4 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Mikael boasts a career +634 point differential, a biggest win margin of 36 points, a clutch 57% win rate in three-game matches (12/21).

Mikael has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Janice Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (17 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Srineet Atluri (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (15 total events in BD/U15, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Kayden Hossain — 4 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

Mikael is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Mikael is still ascending.

Mikael has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mikael has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 17 seeded entries across the career, Mikael consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Mikael has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
mikael_chang.roast:

Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Mikael is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (XD U15), which means Mikael has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. Mikael represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 68 career entries and a 7% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 68 entries worth of material.

The Mikael saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Mikael burst onto the scene with a title in BD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Mikael has delivered on some of those promises: 5 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Take 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Mikael entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U13, XD U13, BD U13). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Mikael does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Mikael has never won anything. But 10 finals losses versus 5 wins means Mikael is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mikael plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mikael can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Mikael has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mikael's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Janice Chen: 17 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 17 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Janice Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Shraddha Prakash has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Mikael entered 3 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Mikael's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Mikael and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Mikael has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mikael is not just losing locally — Mikael is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chang family travels cross-country so Mikael can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mikael has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mikael showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11, which means Mikael traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Mikael entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Mikael paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 68 entries, and 63 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mikael looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mikael's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mikael has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mikael. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The Milena saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Milena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Milena has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Emma Lu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Daniel Xi has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Milena has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Milena has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Milena has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Milena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Milena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Milena entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Milena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Milena keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Milena has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Milena, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Milena. We will be here, taking notes.

mingyang_liu.name: Mingyang Liu
mingyang_liu.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Mingyang Liu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 22 entries in 10 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Mingyang especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Mingyang adapts to anyone:
  • Junwoo Lee (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Mingyang has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Mingyang brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mingyang_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mingyang Liu, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mingyang has 22 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mingyang saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mingyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Mingyang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mingyang's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Darren C Tse has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Mingyang has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Mingyang has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mingyang has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mingyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Mingyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Mingyang entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Mingyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 22 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Mingyang. Mingyang just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Mingyang has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Mingyang. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Mingyang: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

mingyuan_zang.name: Mingyuan Zang
mingyuan_zang.summary:
A California competitor, Mingyuan Zang is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U11, BS U11) puts Mingyuan in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Mingyuan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Mingyuan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U11, BS U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Mingyuan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Eden Wong (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Nina Kuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4)
  • Nova Zhang — 7 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Adrian Zhang — 3 events in BD, best: #4

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Mingyuan is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Mingyuan has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Mingyuan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Mingyuan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mingyuan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mingyuan_zang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mingyuan Zang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mingyuan has 44 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mingyuan saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mingyuan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Mingyuan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mingyuan is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mingyuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mingyuan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Mingyuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mingyuan's texts. The go-to partner is Nova Zhang (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Eden Wong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mingyuan's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Mingyuan has competed 3 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Tournament committees have handed Mingyuan a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Mingyuan has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11, where Mingyuan was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Mingyuan has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mingyuan is not just losing locally — Mingyuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zang family travels cross-country so Mingyuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mingyuan has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mingyuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U11, which means Mingyuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Mingyuan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Mingyuan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 44 entries, and 41 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mingyuan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mingyuan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mingyuan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mingyuan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mishka_mohapatra.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mishka Mohapatra, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Mishka's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Mishka keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Mishka saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mishka plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Mishka has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mishka has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mishka showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U13, which means Mishka traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Mishka entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Mishka paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 12 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Mishka. Mishka just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Mishka has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Mishka. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Mishka: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

mitchell_purnomo.name: Mitchell Purnomo
mitchell_purnomo.summary:
A Washington competitor, Mitchell Purnomo is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Mitchell in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Mitchell has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Mitchell especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 BD) • Junior Nationals top-4 (XD U19) • 2 ORC titles.

Mitchell has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Anvita Jamsandekar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Nicholas Volfbeyn (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #7, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Mitchell has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mitchell has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Mitchell consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Mitchell has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
mitchell_purnomo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mitchell Purnomo, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 57 career entries in 20 tournaments, Mitchell has 4 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 13 entries where Mitchell drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 57 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Mitchell saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Mitchell burst onto the scene with a title in BS U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Mitchell has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Mitchell promised and what Mitchell has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Mitchell actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mitchell is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mitchell experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mitchell has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NW Oregon OLC, where Mitchell entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Mitchell does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Mitchell has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 4 wins means Mitchell is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mitchell plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mitchell can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Mitchell has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mitchell's texts. The go-to partner is Sophia Jia (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Jessica Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Mitchell entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Mitchell's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Mitchell and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Mitchell 8 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Mitchell has been seeded in 6 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Mitchell takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Mitchell. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Mitchell has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mitchell is not just losing locally — Mitchell is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Purnomo family travels cross-country so Mitchell can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mitchell has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mitchell showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Mitchell traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Mitchell entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Mitchell paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 53 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mitchell looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mitchell's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mitchell has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mitchell. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
