mithra_sellammal_raja.name: Mithra Sellammal Raja
mithra_sellammal_raja.summary:
A Texas competitor, Mithra Sellammal Raja is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GD U11 - Position 5-8) puts Mithra in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Mithra is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U11 - Position 5-8).
mithra_sellammal_raja.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mithra Sellammal Raja, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mithra has 3 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mithra saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mithra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Mithra has competed 3 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Mithra is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Mithra wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Mithra, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

mithra_senthil_raja.name: Mithra Senthil Raja
mithra_senthil_raja.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Mithra Senthil Raja has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Mithra's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Mithra is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Mithra is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Mithra has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mithra has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Mithra is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
mithra_senthil_raja.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mithra Senthil Raja, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mithra has 58 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mithra saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mithra's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Mithra actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mithra is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mithra experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mithra has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Mithra does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Mithra has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 4 wins means Mithra is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mithra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Mithra is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Mithra and suddenly the wins start appearing — 4 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Mithra needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Mithra has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mithra's texts. The go-to partner is Ayaaz Muhammed Baig (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ayaaz Muhammed Baig has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mithra's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Mithra has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Mithra has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Mithra takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in GS U11, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Mithra. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Mithra of is playing it safe. Mithra has entered older age groups at 7 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Mithra has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Mithra has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mithra is not just losing locally — Mithra is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Raja family travels cross-country so Mithra can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mithra has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mithra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Mithra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Mithra entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Mithra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 58 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mithra looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mithra's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mithra has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mithra. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

mithun_gopinath.name: Mithun Gopinath
mithun_gopinath.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Mithun Gopinath has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 2 titles across 29 tournaments and 77 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Mithun keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Mithun is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Mithun is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Mithun has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 22 different players across the career:
  • Haripriya Tati (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Mukil Nambikumar (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Mithun has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mithun has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Mithun consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Mithun is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

miura_yan_faye_goh.name: Miura Yan Faye Goh
miura_yan_faye_goh.summary:
A Texas competitor, Miura Yan Faye Goh is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Miura competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Miura has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Chaturveda Samhitha Shanigaram (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Vignan Chandrasekaran — 3 events in XD, best: #3

Miura has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Miura brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Miura's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Miura has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Miura has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Miura showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The sport needs people like Miura. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Miura: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

mizuki_ikeuchi.name: Mizuki Ikeuchi
mizuki_ikeuchi.summary:
A Massachusetts competitor, Mizuki Ikeuchi is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in GS U13. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 10 career victories across 12 tournaments, Mizuki has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Mizuki is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Mizuki especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 5 singles titles, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (GS U13) • 3 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Mizuki boasts a clutch 69% win rate in three-game matches (11/16), a career +814 point differential, a commanding 78% win rate across 90 matches.

Mizuki has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Alaina Benjamin (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Leon Luo (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Ethan Yip Ly (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Bella Liu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Mizuki is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Mizuki has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, South, Mizuki isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Mizuki consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mizuki brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

Mizuki has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mizuki showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Mizuki traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Mizuki entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 49th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Mizuki paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 34 entries, and 24 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mizuki looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. The Mohammad saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret MW CRC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mohammad's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mohammad plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 0 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mohammad can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Mohammad has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Shanmukh Ayyalasomayajula (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U19 — we hope Shanmukh Ayyalasomayajula has emotionally recovered.

A Texas competitor, Mokshagna Surpur has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Mokshagna's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Mokshagna is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Mokshagna especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Mokshagna boasts a solid 68% win rate over 107 matches, 62 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, 18 three-game battles — proving Mokshagna is no stranger to the pressure of a decider.

Mokshagna has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Amogh Lanka (current primary partner) — 15 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (15 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Chaturveda Samhitha Shanigaram (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Shaoxuan Gong (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 7 tournaments. Mokshagna is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Mokshagna has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Mokshagna has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Mokshagna consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Mokshagna brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
mokshagna_surpur.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mokshagna Surpur, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mokshagna has 48 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 12% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mokshagna saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mokshagna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Mokshagna experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Mokshagna has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Fortius South CRC, where Mokshagna entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (XD U13, BD U13, BS U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Mokshagna does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Mokshagna has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 6 wins means Mokshagna is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mokshagna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mokshagna can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Mokshagna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mokshagna's texts. The go-to partner is Amogh Lanka (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Amogh Lanka has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mokshagna's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Mokshagna has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Mokshagna of is playing it safe. Mokshagna has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Mokshagna has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Mokshagna has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mokshagna is not just losing locally — Mokshagna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Surpur family travels cross-country so Mokshagna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mokshagna has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mokshagna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Mokshagna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Mokshagna entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Mokshagna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 48 entries, and 42 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mokshagna looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mokshagna's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mokshagna has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mokshagna. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Molly is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
molly_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Molly Chen, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Molly has 35 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Molly saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Molly's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Molly plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Molly is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Molly and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Molly needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Molly has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Molly's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lanting Jiang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lanting Jiang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Timothy Yung-An Chuang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Molly's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Molly has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Molly is not just losing locally — Molly is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Molly can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Molly has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Molly showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Molly traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Molly entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Molly paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 35 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Molly looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Molly's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Molly has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Molly. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Morgan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Morgan's texts. The go-to partner is Alex Wen (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Surbhi Sakshi has emotionally recovered.

The Li family travels cross-country so Morgan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights.   • Justin Dong (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Mrugakshi has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Mrugakshi has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

mrugakshi_kulkarni.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Mrugakshi Kulkarni, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Mrugakshi has 52 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Mrugakshi saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Mrugakshi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Mrugakshi actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Mrugakshi is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Mrugakshi would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Mrugakshi has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Mrugakshi has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Mrugakshi's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Mrugakshi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Mrugakshi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Mrugakshi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Mrugakshi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Wayne Chien: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Wayne Chien has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Wayne Chien has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Mrugakshi's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Mrugakshi has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Mrugakshi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Mrugakshi is not just losing locally — Mrugakshi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kulkarni family travels cross-country so Mrugakshi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Mrugakshi has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Mrugakshi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15, which means Mrugakshi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Mrugakshi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Mrugakshi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 52 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Mrugakshi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Mrugakshi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Mrugakshi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Mrugakshi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

muyang_li.name: Muyang Li
muyang_li.summary:
Hailing from California, Muyang Li has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Muyang's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Muyang competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Muyang has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Muyang has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Muyang consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Muyang brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
muyang_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Muyang Li, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Muyang has 53 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Muyang saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Muyang would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Muyang has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Muyang has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Muyang's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Muyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 53 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Muyang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Muyang's texts. The go-to partner is Lydia Ng (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Stanley Xing has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Muyang has shown up 7 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Muyang is not just losing locally — Muyang is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Muyang can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Muyang has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Muyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17, which means Muyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Muyang entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Muyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 53 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Muyang. Muyang just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Muyang has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Muyang. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Muyang: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
